courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: History for Today
courses.description: This course teaches the art and science of historical research. It enhances Innovation and Design (I&D) by encouraging students to think out of the box, with critical thinking from multiple angles, with high ethical standard, and be innovative and creative. It enhances Global Citizenship and Social Enterprisingness (GC&SE) by fostering students’ ability and readiness to embark on creative and new ventures of social value in a global context, in line with global and ethical values. The topics will be adjusted every year based on a flexible and student-oriented approach. Details of the curriculum are available in the course website of the Department of History.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the course, students will:

a) have acquired satisfactory understanding of the art and science of historical research.

b) have enhanced capability of Innovation and Design (I&D) by developing critical thinking.

c) have enhanced capability of Global Citizenship and Social Enterprisingness (GC&SE) by fostering ability and readiness to embark on creative and new ventures of social value in a global context, in line with global and ethical values.

d) have enhanced writing, reading and communication skills.
courses.course_syllabus:
The course syllabus might be revised annually and, where necessary, during the semester. For updated syllabus, please refer to the course website of the Department of History and also to the announcement of the teachers of the course. Below is one of the recent syllabi.

(1) Introduction

(2) Methodology: Paul Cohen’s History in Three Keys.

(3) Social History: Natalia Davis’ The Return of Martin Guerre

(4) Social History: Robert Danton’s The Great Cat Massacre

(5) Economic History: Famine, state and market in late imperial China

(6) Economic History: Currency and finance in late imperial China

(7) Legal History: The Case of Macheng

(8) Legal History: The Case of the Rent Grain Bucket

(9) Historical Anthrpology: James Watson’s finding of T’ien Hou

(10) Historical Anthrpology: David Faure’s Emperor and Ancestor

(11) Semester Paper Workshop: Footnote

(12) Semester Paper Workshop: Historical sources and analysis

(13) Semester Paper Workshop: Narrative and structure

(1) 序論

(2) 方法論：柯文《歷史三調》

(3) 社會史：戴維斯《馬丁蓋爾回歸》

(4) 社會史：丹頓《貓之大屠殺》

(5) 經濟史：中國歷史上的饑荒、國家、市場

(6) 經濟史：中國歷史上的貨幣與金融

(7) 法制史：麻城案

(8) 法制史：倉斗案

(9) 歷史人類學：華琛有關天后的研究

(10) 歷史人類學：科大衛《皇帝和祖宗》

(11) 學期論文工作坊：注釋

(12) 學期論文工作坊：史料與分析

(13) 學期論文工作坊：敘述與結構
courses.required_readings: Cohen, Paul History in Three Keys
courses.recommended_readings:

Watson, James “Standardizing the gods: the promotion of T’ien Hou (‘Empress of Heaven’) along the South China Coast”

courses.description: This course examines the macro history of China by way of studying changes of traditional ideology, historical geography, state institutions and political reforms.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken UGEA2223 or 2230
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the course, students will:

(1) Learn how to examine the macro history of China;
(2) Understand changes in Chinese traditional ideology, historical geography, state institutions and political reforms

修讀本科後，學生當能：
(1) 學懂以宏觀的視野，分析中國二千年的大歷史。

(2) 透視中國傳統思想、歷史地理、經濟演變和政治。
courses.course_syllabus:
(1) Ideology and Politics in Ancient China

(2) Geography and History in Medieval Time

(3) Institutions and Society in Ming-Qing Period

(4) Transformation of China in Modern Age

(1) 古代思想與政治

(2) 中古地理和歷史

(3) 明清制度與社會

(4) 近代中國的轉型

courses.description: This course examines the main historical events in Western Civilization from Mesopotamia to the twentieth century. Major historical themes of the West and their significance are introduced in order to attain a macro-historical understanding of Western Civilization.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken GENA2112 or UGEC1221
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the course, students will:

1. Be able to have a better understanding of the tradition and transformation of the western history and culture;
2. Be able to critically think about related issues of the west;
3. Be able to gain a basic understanding of the history and development of the world as a whole.

修讀本科後，學生當能：
1. 更了解西方歷史和文化之傳統與變革；

2. 批判思考西方相關之問題；

3. 基本了解整體世界歷史之發展。
courses.course_syllabus:
- Introduction: What is history?/The Ancient Near East: Mesopotamia & Egypt

- The Ancient Greek Literature: Aesop’s Fables & Greek Tragedies (Homer and Sophocles)

- The Greek polis (Athens and Sparta)

- The Greek Philosophy: Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle

- The Rise of Christianity

- The Fall of the Roman Empire

- Medieval Feudalism

- Medieval Islam

- Medieval Church and Empire

- Medieval England

- From the Age of Faith to the Age of Reason/Enlightenment

- The Century of Revolutions: from the French Revolution, 1789 to the 1848 Revolution

- Twentieth-Century Europe: the Two World Wars & “The Political Collapse of Europe”

什麼是歷史？古代近東
- 古希臘文學

- 希臘城邦

- 古希臘哲學

- 基督教之興起

- 羅馬帝國之衰亡

- 中古封建

- 中古伊斯蘭教

- 中古政教鬥爭

- 中古英國

- 從信仰時代到理性時代

- 革命的十八、十九世紀

- 兩次大戰及「歐洲的政治式微」

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Introduction to Public History: Theory and Practice
courses.description: The field of public history has been expanding so rapidly since its inception in the 1970s that even public historians find it difficult to agree on a precise definition for public history. This course adopts a broad definition, which seeks to understand public history as a discipline in which historians practice history with a public audience in mind, and as a medium through which the general public acquire a sense of the past. Major topics examined in this course include museums, heritage, films, public monuments and tourism, etc. Local and global examples are used.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. understand the roles history and historians play in various public settings in Hong Kong and in other countries

2. analyze the popular use of history in everyday life

3. gain awareness of the ethical concerns within the discipline of public history

1. 理解歷史及歷史學者在香港及其他國家在公共領域扮演的角色

2. 分析民眾在日常生活中如何利用歷史

3. 反省公眾史學所牽涉的倫理問題
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Public History: A Changing Discipline

2. Bridging the Gap between Historians and the Public

3. Popular Uses of History

4. Presenting the Past: Museums

5. Power Politics of World Heritage

6. Changing Definitions of Heritage: From Tangible to Intangible

7. Marketing History and Heritage: A Local Perspective

8. Marketing History and Heritage: A Global Perspective

9. History and the Mass Media

10. Projecting History in Public Space: Monuments and Power

11. Public History and Collective Memory

12. Gender and Public History

13. Conclusion: The Future of Public History

1. 公眾史學的定義

2. 如何拉近歷史學者與公眾的距離?

3. 大眾如何應用歷史?

4. 博物館如何展陳過去?

5. 世界遺產中的權力政治

6. 物質與非物質文化遺產

8. 文化產業與文物旅遊: 國際視角

9. 大眾傳媒中的歷史

10. 紀念碑上的歷史與權力

11. 公眾歷史與「集體記憶」

12. 公眾歷史與性別關係

13. 公眾歷史的未來

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: History for Tomorrow: Technology and the Humanities
courses.description: Computer games, apps and AI are everywhere in the lives of CUHK students. These technologies are useful in our everyday interactions, but they can also help us be better historians and humanists. In this introductory course, you will learn about the exciting field of Digital Humanities (especially Digital History), which applies all manner of technologies to scholarly questions. The course will introduce the resources available at CUHK for digital history and humanities (DS Lab, VR Studio, 3-Printing space, etc.). It will also involve the study of some exciting applications of tools, like VR, text analysis, 3-D modeling and printing, historical mapping, etc. At the end of the course, students will have a solid foundation for further studies in digital history and digital humanities more broadly.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Consider the relationship between technology and humanities studies (especially history)

2. Learn about the resources at CUHK for digital scholarship (including UL DS Lab, VR Studio, etc.)

3. Acquire practical skills in digital humanities (especially digital history) that will be useful for more advanced courses

4. Apply these practical skills to address specific scholarly questions in a variety of historical periods and locations

5. Collaborate with other students to create a final project

1. 思考科技與人文學科（特別是歷史學）的關係

2. 暸解香港中大大學的數碼學術研究資源（包括圖書館的數碼學術研究室、虛擬實境空間等）

3. 掌握實用的數位人文學（特別是數位史學）技能，讓同學能夠在更高階段的科目中應用

4. 利用這些的實用技能處理不同歷史時期與地方的學術問題

5. 與同學合作完成研究計劃
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction: Humanities and Technology?

2. History for Today, History for Tomorrow? The Origins of the Digital Scholarship

3. Resources at CUHK: DS Lab, etc.

4. Key questions in the Digital Humanities

5. Reading with Technology I: English texts

6. Reading with Technology II: Literary Sinitic (Classical Chinese)

7. What is a Map?

8. Rebuilding the Pyramids?

9. 3-D Printing

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Historiography (Advanced)
courses.description: This course examines the basic principles and methods of the study of history, particularly in the twentieth century. Topics include epistemology of history, interdisciplinary approaches to historical research, the criticism of source materials, historical analysis and explanation, the craftsmanship of historical research, and the major new methodologies and perspectives.
courses.learning_outcomes:
At the end of the course, students should be able to achieve the following objectives:

1. Gain a solid training in the craftsmanship of academic presentation and historiographical writing;

2. Identify, understand, and critically analyze major schools of thoughts in historiography;

3. Demonstrate profound understanding and critical analysis of various historical archives.

學生完成課程後，將能：

1. 在作學術報告及寫作歷史學論文方面得到扎實的訓練；

2. 辨別、理解、及批判現代歷史學發展中的不同流派的學說和主張；

3. 充分理解及深入分析展示各種史料。
courses.course_syllabus:
(1) Introduction: Historiography, histroians, and the passage of time

(2) The production of historical knowledge

(3) History as a modern academic discipline

(4) Modern historical writing and historian's craft

(5) Marxist historiography and the French Annales School

(6) From social history to new cultural history (I)

(7) From social history to new cultural history (II)

(8) National history, global history, and comparative studies

(9) Doing public history, field work, and oral history

(10) The transformation of modern Chinese historiography (I)

(11) The transformation of modern Chinese historiography (II)

(12) Paradigm shifts in overseas historical writing of China (I)

(13) Paradigm shifts in overseas historical writing of China (II)

第二講　歷史（知識）的生產與製作

第七講　從社會史到新文化史（二）

第八講　國別史、全球史與比較史的視野

第九講　公眾史、田野調查與口述歷史

courses.description: The ancient Mediterranean world (c. 800 BCE-250 CE) is often seen as the fountainhead of “Western” culture, but what was it really like? This course aims to help students understand the core contributions (philosophical, political, literary, etc.) of the ancient Mediterranean world to later periods of “Western” history, and its relationship to the rest of the world (Han China, India, ancient Near East, etc.). To do so, we will focus on a series of “moments” (and the related sources), which either had a particular influence on later periods or display the interconnectedness of the ancient world. It will also include a visit to the University Library to examine reproductions of ancient Western material culture.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Develop an understanding of the earliest period of Western History.

2. Understand the historical relationship between what we call the West and other parts of the world

3. Improve written and oral skills in academic English

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Introduction to World History: Modern Era
courses.description: This course will focus on Western and World History from 1500 until the present, with a focus on the global transformations that brought previously isolated parts of the world into contact with each other. It will begin with the emergence of Europe on the world stage during the sixteenth century with its Age of Exploration and its expansion into the Americas. It will then proceed through the main dynamics of world history both within The West and in its interactions with the peoples of the Americas, the Middle East, and Africa-through subsequent waves of colonization, the rise of industrialization, the World Wars, the Cold War, and the decolonization that took place during the 1950s and 1960s, through to the end of the Cold War and the emergence of the modern world order.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To teach students the fundamentals of modern world history (post-1500): key dates, events, places, states, beliefs, innovations, etc.

2. To familiarize students with several key historiographical debates and several methodological approaches world historians have used.

3. To teach students how to work with primary sources.

1. 讓同學掌握1500年以來西方的歷史梗概：關鍵日期、事件、地點、國家、信仰、科技發明。

2. 讓同學掌握史學界對西方現代歷史的主要論辯和研究方法。

3. 教導同學如何利用原始史料進行研究。

courses.required_readings:
The Reading will be a combination of one textbook as a reference work/overview, and primary sources to familiarize students with both the content of the era and the skills involved in interpreting primary sources.

Secondary sources will be decided by the instructor to keep up with recent trends in historiography but could include works like John Darwin, After Tamerlane; Peter Stearns et. al., World Civilizations; or Elizabeth Sundermann, et. al., Global Themes in World History since 1500.
courses.recommended_readings: Primary sources to complement the textbook could include Machiavelli, The Prince; Martin Luther's 95 Theses; Bartolome de las Casas, The Destruction of the Indes; The Political Testament of the Cardinal Richelieu; Jean-Jacques Rousseau, The Social Contract; Thomas Jefferson, The Declaration of Independence; The Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen; Bolivar, Message to the Congress of Angostura; Engels, Conditions of the Working Class in England; John Stuart Mill, On Liberty; Darwin, Voyage of the Beagle, or Origin of the Species; Erich Maria Remarque, All Quiet on the Western Front; Lenin, What Is To Be Done?; Mussolini, What is Fascism?; Wiesel, Night; Fanon, The Wretched of the Earth; Havel, The Power of the Powerless.

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Oral History: Methods and Practices
courses.description: This course aims at investigating the principles and methods of oral history with the focus on oral history interview techniques. This course introduces the development of oral history, interview techniques as well as application and analysis of orated data.
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Understand the principles and methods of oral history.
* Through practices, students are expected to collect and analyse the data from interviews and master the principles and methods of oral history.
* 明暸口述歷史的原理與方法；
* 透過實踐，讓同學收集和分析口述歷史資料，從而掌握口述歷史的原理與方法。
courses.course_syllabus:
Part I: Theories

1. What is oral history?

王明珂，〈誰的歷史：自傳、傳記與口述歷史的社會記憶本質〉，《思與言》，第34卷3期，頁18-31。

Portelli, Alessandro. (1991). The Death of Luigi Trastulli and other Stories: Forms and Meaning in Oral History. Albany, N.Y.: State University of New York Press, pp. 45-58.

2. The history, values and limitations of oral history

楊祥銀，〈當代美國的口述史學〉，載王俊義、丁東主編，《口述歷史》第1輯，北京：中國社會科學出版社，2003 年9 月，初版，頁276‐283。

熊月之，〈口述史的價值〉，《史林》，2000 年第3 期，頁1‐7。

3. Personal and collective memories

保羅．湯保遜 (Thompson, P.)，單方明等譯，《過去的聲音：口述歷史》，香港：牛津大學出版社，1989，頁139-153。

Connerton, Paul. (1989). How Societies Remember, Cambridge : Cambridge University Press, pp. 6-40.

Part II: Practice

4. Planning on oral history project: objective, theme and target informant

張朋園，〈如何推動口述歷史計畫：為中國民國護理學會推動該一計劃演講〉，《口述歷史》第9 期，台北：中央研究院近代史研究所，1999 年，頁187-201。

唐諾里齊(Donald Ritchie)著；王芝芝譯，《大家來做口述歷史》，台北：遠流出版社，1997。

5. Question design and technique of interview

Ronald J. Grele. (2011). History and the Languages of History in the Oral History Interview: Who Answers Whose Questions and Why?” In Eva M. McMahan, and Kim L. Rogers (eds.). Interactive Oral History Interviewing. New York and London: Routledge, pp.1-18.

Sheftel, Anna and Stacey Zembrzucki, eds. (2013). Oral History off the Record: Towards an Ethnography of Practice. New York: Palgrave, pp. 81-96, 223-237.

6. Ethical and legal issues

Dougherty, Jack and Candace Simpson. (2012) “Who Owns Oral History? A Creative Commons Solution.” In Doug Boyd (et. al.) Oral History in the Digital Age. Washington, D.C.: Institute of Museum and Library Services.

Neuenschwander, John A. (2014) “Legal Release Agreements.” In A Guide to Oral History and the Law (3rd edition). New York: Oxford University Press, pp. 3-19.

7. Transcription and archiving

沈懷玉，〈口述訪問稿與資料的整理〉，《近代中國》，第149 期(2002年6 月)，頁8–16。

林巧敏，〈典藏記憶：檔案館口述歷史工作之規劃與實施〉，《檔案季刊》，第11 卷第4 期(2012 年12 月)，頁121–132。

Fogerty, James E. (2007) “Oral History and Archives: Documenting Context.” In Thomas L. Charlton, Lois E. Myers, and Rebecca Sharpless. (eds.) History of Oral History: Foundations and Methodology. Lanham, MD: AltaMira Press, pp. 197-211.

Part III: Analysis and Research

8. Whose history? Sharing authority between interviewer and interviewee

Thomson, Alistair (2003) Introduction - Sharing Authority: Oral History and the Collaborative Process. Oral History Review, 30 (1): 23-26.

Kennedy, Elizabeth Lapovsky. (1995) “Telling Tales: Oral History and the Construction of PreStonewall Lesbian History.” Radical History Review. 1995(62): 59-79.

9. Analysis, proof and interpretation

翁秀琪，〈多元典範衝擊下傳播研究方法省思：從口述歷史在傳播研究中的應用談起〉，《新聞學研究》，63(2000):9‐33。

陳三井，〈口述歷史的理論及史料價值〉，《當代》，125(1998)，頁102‐109。

許雪姬，〈史學與史料：談口述歷史與歷史研究〉，收入《兩岸發展史學術演講專輯．第五輯》(2009)，頁35‐54。

10. Social functions of oral history: reconstruction of history, social movement and empowerment

梁妃儀等編，《協助社群認同發展的口述歷史實踐：綜合理論與實務的操作手冊》，台北：唐山出版社，2003。

葉漢明，《口述史的性別維度：從工作與家庭對香港製衣業男、女工人的意義切入探討》，香港：香港中文大學香港亞太研究所，2014。

11. Conclusion

第一部分：理論

1. 何謂口述歷史

王明珂，〈誰的歷史：自傳、傳記與口述歷史的社會記憶本質〉，《思與言》，第34卷3期，頁18-31。

Portelli, Alessandro. (1991). The Death of Luigi Trastulli and other Stories: Forms and Meaning in Oral History. Albany, N.Y.: State University of New York Press, pp. 45-58.

2. 口述歷史的發展、價值與局限

楊祥銀，〈當代美國的口述史學〉，載王俊義、丁東主編，《口述歷史》第1輯，北京：中國社會科學出版社，2003 年9 月，初版，頁276‐283。

熊月之，〈口述史的價值〉，《史林》，2000 年第3 期，頁1‐7。

3. 個人與集體記憶

保羅．湯保遜 (Thompson, P.)，單方明等譯：《過去的聲音：口述歷史》，香港：牛津大學出版社，1989，頁139-153。

Connerton, Paul. (1989). How Societies Remember, Cambridge : Cambridge University Press, pp. 6-40.

第二部分：實踐

4. 訪談計劃：目標、主題、受訪對象

張朋園，〈如何推動口述歷史計畫：為中國民國護理學會推動該一計劃演講〉，《口述歷史》第9 期，台北：中央研究院近代史研究所，1999 年，頁187-201。

唐諾里齊(Donald Ritchie)著；王芝芝譯，《大家來做口述歷史》，台北：遠流出版社，1997。

5. 問題設計與訪談技巧

Ronald J. Grele. (2011). History and the Languages of History in the Oral History Interview: Who Answers Whose Questions and Why?” In Eva M. McMahan, and Kim L. Rogers (eds.). Interactive Oral History Interviewing. New York and London: Routledge, pp.1-18.

Sheftel, Anna and Stacey Zembrzucki, eds. (2013). Oral History off the Record: Towards an Ethnography of Practice. New York: Palgrave, pp. 81-96, 223-237.

6. 道德、倫理與法律問題

Dougherty, Jack and Candace Simpson. (2012) “Who Owns Oral History? A Creative Commons Solution.” In Doug Boyd (et. al.) Oral History in the Digital Age. Washington, D.C.: Institute of Museum and Library Services.

Neuenschwander, John A. (2014) “Legal Release Agreements.” In A Guide to Oral History and the Law (3rd edition). New York: Oxford University Press, pp. 3-19.

7. 整理與儲存

沈懷玉，〈口述訪問稿與資料的整理〉，《近代中國》，第149 期(2002年6 月)，頁8–16。

林巧敏，〈典藏記憶：檔案館口述歷史工作之規劃與實施〉，檔案季刊》，第11 卷第4 期(2012 年12 月)，頁121–132。

Fogerty, James E. (2007) “Oral History and Archives: Documenting Context.” In Thomas L. Charlton, Lois E. Myers, and Rebecca Sharpless. (eds.) History of Oral History: Foundations and Methodology. Lanham, MD: AltaMira Press, pp. 197-211.

第三部分：分析與研究

8. 誰的歷史：受訪者與訪問員的共享權限

Thomson, Alistair (2003) Introduction - Sharing Authority: Oral History and the Collaborative Process. Oral History Review, 30 (1): 23-26.

Kennedy, Elizabeth Lapovsky. (1995) “Telling Tales: Oral History and the Construction of PreStonewall Lesbian History.” Radical History Review. 1995(62): 59-79.

9. 分析、求證與解讀

翁秀琪，〈多元典範衝擊下傳播研究方法省思：從口述歷史在傳播研究中的應用談起〉，《新聞學研究》，63(2000):9‐33。

陳三井，〈口述歷史的理論及史料價值〉，《當代》，125(1998)，頁102‐109。

許雪姬，〈史學與史料：談口述歷史與歷史研究〉，收入《兩岸發展史學術演講專輯．第五輯》(2009)，頁35‐54。

10. 口述歷史的社會功能：歷史重構、社會運動、社群充權

梁妃儀等編，《協助社群認同發展的口述歷史實踐：綜合理論與實務的操作手冊》，台北：唐山出版社，2003。

11. 總結

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: China Today
courses.description: This course examines prominent issues in contemporary China and its development in the world system from a historical perspective.
courses.learning_outcomes:
修畢本科後，學生當能：
1. 明白廿世紀七十年代至廿一世紀初之間中國大陸在改革和現代化過程中所經歷的重大變化。

2. 認識有關中國當代史的各種概念、觀點和角度、研究現況、方法、史料、及重要著述。

3. 透過上課、導修、閱讀及功課，掌握中國當代史的發展脈絡，及對重要問題作出批判分析。

4. 通過口頭和文字報告表述以上有關史識。

5. 在作業中展示對中國當代史的綜合、說明和解釋能力以及研究潛力。
courses.course_syllabus:
講授重點及目標
本科重點在探討廿世紀七十年代至廿一世紀初之間中國大陸在改革和現代化過程中所經歷的重大變化，涉及黨國權力結構、國家與社會、中央與地方的關係、政治文化、經濟發展以及中國在世界體系中的地位等問題，兼及當代台灣社會的轉型及其啟示。除發展脈絡的闡述及重要問題的分析外，本科尚注重各種概念、觀點和角度及重要著述的評介。學生可透過上課、導修、閱讀及功課，掌握以上課程內容，並作出分析研判。
講授專題及閱讀材料
1 概述：有關當代中國的解釋模式、微觀分析與批判反思

2 “毛澤東時代”的影響與變化之源

3 放映錄像帶及堂上討論：文革後遺症

4 合法性基礎的轉換與“鄧小平時代”的歷史意義

5 國家與社會關係的悖論

6 “改革開放”的難題與“六四天安門事件”的根源

7 放映錄像帶及堂上討論：改革反思

8 “後鄧小平時代”的中央與地方

9 第三、四代領導層與國內外衝擊

10 來自海峽對岸的挑戰

11 台灣經驗與黨國模式的蛻變

12 放映錄像帶及堂上討論：台灣啟示

13 體制轉型與兩岸政治社會變化

14 總結：民主化及其他 ── “新權威主義”、“社會主義民主” 、 “後共產主義、“資本主義世界體系” 或第三條道路?

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Transformations in Tang-Song China
courses.description: This course intends to examine the political, diplomatic, social, economic and cultural transformations from Tang to Song China, with emphasis on the political centralization by autocratic monarchy, the structural changes of society and economy as well as the evolving socio-cultural life. It will enable students to master the basic concepts, knowledge and empirical methods for the study of the theory of the T’ang-Song transformation in multiple perspectives through PowerPoint lectures, interactive tutorials, primary text readings and documentary film appreciation.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction to historical sources for the Sui, T’ang and Song dynasties

It introduces various kinds of sources for the three dynasties including official histories, biographies, chronologies, institutional histories, encyclopaedia, unofficial histories, fictions, unearthed relics and documents, etc.

2. Review of recent studies on the periodization based on the T’ang-Song transformation

It reviews the different periodization based on the transformation by the Kyoto school and Tokyo school in Japan and other scholars in China, Taiwan and U.S.

3. The centralized administration system under the legal codes (ritsuryo) in the T’ang dynasty and the development from equal-field system to the manorial system

It demonstrates the evolution of penal codex, administrative codex, amendments to penal codex and enforcement regulations, the T’ang administration system under the legal code especially the ideal equal-field system and its reality, and the development of the manorial system in the late T’ang and Song dynasties.

4. From grain-labour-kind tax system to double tax system

It examines the grain-labour-kind tax system and its defects, the advantages of the double tax system in the T’ang dynasty, and the remarkable changes in the latter system from the late T’ang to the Song dynasty, and the historical significance of the transformation from the former system to the latter.

5. Collapse of the bureaucratic and military systems under the legal codes (ritsuryo), and formation and institutionalization of various commissioners (I)

It explains the operation, features and breakdown of the bureaucratic system and the garrison militia system, and the rise of various commissioners in the T’ang dynasty.

6. Collapse of the bureaucratic and military systems under the legal codes (ritsuryo) and formation and institutionalization of various commissioners (II)

It investigates the power expansion and institutionalization of the state fiscal commissioners, military affairs commissioners and Hanlin academicians from the late T’ang to the Song dynasty, and the development of the threefold parallel official system which consisted of vane ranks (guan), vane titles (zhi) and temporary ordinances (chaiqian) to control the recalcitrant military commissioners in the Song dynasty.

7. The policy of power centralization with emphasis on Confucianism and the idea of "civil first, martial second"

It analyses the relationships between the military commissionaries and the change of regimes in the late T’ang and Five Dynasties, and the policy of power centralization with emphasis on Confucianism and the idea of "civil first, martial second".

8. From power struggles to conflicts between different political factions: Evolution of court factionalism in the T’ang-Song period

It examines the power struggles headed by the imperial families before the reign of Emperor Shunzhong in the T’ang dynasty and the formation and development of political factionalism from the reign to the Song dynasty.

9. Class system and the decline of lowly people and growth of free people

It analyses the class system which consisted of the emperor, aristocracy, officials, common people and lowly people in the T’ang dynasty. It also traces the process of the breakdown of lowly people system and the rise of free people to demonstrate the collapse of class system in the T’ang Song period.

10. Decline of aristocracy and rise of literati

It shows the rise and fall of aristocracy in the T’ang dynasty and the growth and uniqueness of literati in the Song dynasty.

11. Southward shift of the national economic center

It analyses the gradual shift of the national economic center from North China to South China especially the regions of the middle and lower reaches of Yangtze River in the period from the An Lu-shan rebellion to the Song dynasty and its crucial factors.

12. Rise of commodity-monetary economy and urban revolution

It demonstrates the transformation of natural economy into commodity-monetary economy in the T’ang dynasty by which the walled-ward system broke down and guilds flourished. It also shows that the commercial and social functions of cities became more active which made urban life focused on the lower classes.

13. Rise of vernacular literatures: From the T’ang popular lecture to the Song story telling

It demonstrates the forms and contents of the popular lectures and the dance with music in the T’ang dynasty, and the evolution of the popular lecture into the story telling in the Song dynasty.

14. The changing role of the Sui, T’ang and Song Empires in the international order of East Asia

It explores the diplomatic relations of the Sui, T’ang and Song dynasties with the neighbouring countries under the canonized system and tributary state system, and particularly the changing role of the three Empires in the international order of East Asia.

1. 唐宋史料入門

本課簡介唐宋時代的各類原始史料，包括紀傳體正史、傳記、編年、政典、會要、類書、雜史、小說、出土古物及文獻等。

2. 「唐宋變革」的歷史分期研究回顧

本課回顧日本的京都學派、東京學派及中國、臺灣、美國等地的學者對唐宋變革期的時代分期理論。

3. 唐朝律令體制及均田制轉向莊園制之發展

本課說明律、令、格、式制度的形成發展、唐朝律令體制尤其均田制的理想與運作實況，以及唐朝後期至宋朝莊園制的演變發展。

4. 從租庸調制到兩稅制之演變

本課考察唐朝的租庸調制及其弊病、兩稅制的優點和其制在唐宋之間的內容變化，以及兩種稅制更替的歷史意義。

5. 律令官僚兵制崩壞與唐宋使職之興起及中樞機構化(1)

本課探討唐朝律令體制下的官僚制和府兵制的運作、特徵及崩潰情況，以及各種使職之興起。

6. 律令官僚兵制崩壞與唐宋使職之興起及中樞機構化(2)

本課探討晚唐至宋朝三司使、樞密院、翰林學士的職權擴展和制度化趨勢，又說明宋朝針對晚唐五代方鎮擅權而產生的官、職、差遣分合發展。

7. 唐末五代政局之反動－宋朝的強幹弱枝及崇儒右文政策

本課分析晚唐五代方鎮跟中央政權的統治更替之關係，以及宋朝的強幹弱枝、右文抑武政策。

8. 黨爭型態之轉變－從權力鬥爭到政見之爭

本課考察唐德宗朝以前以皇室貴族為首的統治集團內部派系權力鬥爭，以及順宗朝至五代、宋朝政見黨爭的形成與發展。

9. 身分制社會與賤民、良民佃戶之消長

本課剖析由皇帝、貴族、官僚、良民、賤民組成的唐代身分制社會，又考察賤民制破壞與自由民階層誕生的過程，以說明身分制社會崩壞的一個重要方面。

10. 門閥衰落與士大夫之興起

本課說明唐朝門閥士族的形成、發展及沒落，以及宋朝士大夫的興起及其獨特性格。

11. 國家經濟重心之南移

本課分析唐安史之亂以後至宋朝期間國家經濟重心從北方逐漸南移至長江中下游地區的過程，並說明導致南移的重要因素。

12. 商品貨幣經濟之興盛與城市規制之變革

本課說明唐朝的自然經濟向商品貨幣經濟轉型，坊市制度崩壞，行會盛行，城市的商業、社會功能逐漸提升，城市生活轉以社會下層為中心。

13. 通俗文學之誕生－從唐代俗講到宋元說話

本課探討唐代俗講和大麯的體例及內容，並說明俗講在後來轉變成為宋代的說話。

14. 東亞國際秩序與帝國地位之轉變

本課考察隋、唐、宋三朝透過冊封與朝貢制度與鄰近外族國家建立之關係，尤其三朝在東亞國際秩序中所扮演的角色和地位之轉變。

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: History of Taiwan
courses.description: The aim of the course is to provide an opportunity for students to understand the history of Taiwan from prehistory to modern period. The colonization polices of Dutch and Japanese will be discussed in the course, so as the political and social changes after the coming of Kuomintang.
courses.learning_outcomes:
This course provides a general background for students to:

a. broaden the knowledge of modern china;
b. broaden the cross-strait political knowledge;
c. comprehend the general history of Taiwan.

a. 加深對近代中國的認識。

b. 加深兩岸政局的理解。

c. 使學生對台灣史有一般的認識。
courses.course_syllabus:
1 前言

2 史前台灣及原住民社會

3 西班牙及荷蘭殖民時期

4 明鄭時期

5 清朝對台政策

6 台灣日治時期

7 國民黨來台

8 戒嚴時期

9 經濟起飛及亞洲四小龍

10 美麗島事件及民進黨的興起

11 兩岸關係的變化

12 台灣多元文化的出現
courses.feedback_evaluation:
- Tailored questionnaire

- Visiting Committee report

- Reflection of teachers

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Walking through Chinese History
courses.description: This is a course on general and macro history of China. Non-local students and exchange students are particularly welcome. This course will explore episodes, events, thoughts and people that determined or changed the course of Chinese history in the past two millennia. Apart from classroom lectures, fieldtrips and other cultural activities in Hong Kong, Macau, or Mainland China might be organized.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for HIstory Majors. Not for student who have taken HIST1001.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After taking this course, students are able to
1. Understand general Chinese History.
2. Examine the continuity between 'traditional' and 'modern' China.
3. Appreciate Chinese cultural and aesthetics

修習完此科後，學生能夠
1. 對中國歷史有一定程度的理解。
2. 比較古代及現代中國的傳承。
3. 欣賞中國的文化及美學。
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction: The Study of Chinese History
2. Confucianism in Ancient China
3. The 'Great Unity'
4. Han Dynasty and the Silk Road
5. 'The Golden Era' of Tang
6. The Neo-Confucianism in the Song Dynasty
7. Mongol in China
8. Ming Dynasty and the rise of material culture
9. Qing Dynasty and the encounters with the West
10. Revolution and the Chinese Civil War
11. PRC (1949-1978)
12. PRC since 1978

1. 中國歷史略論
2. 儒家及古代中國
3. 中國大一統
4. 漢代及絲綢之路
5. 盛唐時代
6. 宋朝及新儒家
7. 蒙古統治下的中國
8. 明朝及精緻文化之萌芽
9. 清朝與西方
10. 革命與國共內戰
11. 當代中國(1949-1978)
12. 當代中國(1978迄今)
courses.feedback_evaluation:
- Course questionnaire
- Visiting Committee report
- Reflection of teachers
courses.required_readings:
1. Introduction: The Study of Chinese History
- Kenneth Pomeranz, The Great Divergence: China, Europe, and the Making of the Modern World Economy (Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press, 2021), pp. 3-30.

2. Confucianism in Ancient China
- Chinese Civilization Centre, City University of Hong Kong, China: Five Thousand Years of History & Civilization. Hong Kong: City University of Hong Kong Press, 2007, pp. 220-242.

3. The 'Great Unity'
- J. Fairbank & M. Goldman, China: A New History (Enlarged Edition). Cambridge, MA: The Belknap Press of Harvard University Press, 1992, pp. 26-71.

4. Han Dynasty and the Silk Road
- M. Lewis, The Early Chinese Empires: Qin and Han. Cambridge, MA: The Belknap Press of Harvard University Press, 2007, pp. 128-154.

5. 'The Golden Era' of Tang
- M. Lewis, China’s Cosmopolitan Empire: The Tang Dynasty. Cambridge, MA: The Belknap Press of Harvard University Press, 2009, pp. 30-57, 85-120.

6. The Neo-Confucianism in the Song Dynasty
- D. Kuhn, The Age of Confucian Rule: The Song Transformation of China. Cambridge, MA: The Belknap Press of Harvard University Press, 2009, pp. 29-48, 99-119.

7. Mongol in China
- M. Rossabi, A History of China. Malden and Oxford: Wiley Blackwell, 2014, pp. 211-231.

8. Ming Dynasty and the rise of material culture
- E. Mote, Imperial China: 900-1800. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1999, pp. 598-621, 743-775.

9. Qing Dynasty and the encounters with the West
- W. Rowe, China’s Last Empire: The Great Qing. Cambridge, MA: The Belknap Press of Harvard University Press, 2009, pp. 63-89,149-174.

10. China in the Twentieth Century
- J. Spence, The Search for Modern China. New York: W.W. Norton & Company, 1990, pp. 275-333.
courses.recommended_readings:
1. D. Bodde, Essay on Chinese Civilization, Princeton: Princeton University Press, 2014
2. Confucius, The Analects: An illustrated edition, NJ : Princeton University Press. 2018.
3. C. Sanft, Communication and cooperation in early imperial China: publicizing the Qin dynasty, State University of New York Press, 2014.
4. V. Hansen, The Silk Road: a new history, Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2012.
5. M. Lewis, China’s cosmopolitan empire: The Tang dynasty, Cambridge, MA: Belknap Press of Harvard University Press, 2009.
6. C. Hartman., The making of Song dynasty history: sources and narratives, 960-1279 CE, Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2021.
7. T. Brook, The troubled empire : China in the Yuan and Ming dynasties, Cambridge, MA: Belknap Press of Harvard University Press, 2010.
8. P. Perdue., China Marches West : The Qing Conquest of Central Eurasia , Cambridge, MA : Harvard University Press, 2005.

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: State and Society in Ancient China
courses.description: This course examines the historical and cultural development of the pre-Qin periods, with emphasis on the origin of Chinese civilization, the formation of ancient states, the establishment of fengjian (feudal) system of the Zhou, and the mobility of social classes during the Chunqiu and Zhanguo periods.
courses.learning_outcomes:
- Understand the cultural and social history through the study of the history of Pre-Qin period.

- Grasp presentation skills through discussion in tutorials.

- Acquire research skills for the study of historical archives, such as standard histories and archaeological relies etc.

- 瞭解先秦時期的文化及社會發展。

- 透過導修課學習書面及口頭報告的技巧。

- 學習掌握研究歷史的文獻及出土文物的知識。
courses.course_syllabus:
第一章 研究方法與課題
第二章 中國古代文明起源
第三章 國家起源與發展
第四章 商代國家形態與文化
第五章 三星堆與古文明
第六章 天命所歸：周的興起
第七章 國家的形成與封建
第八章 西周的政府組織
第九章 西周社會與物質文明
第十章 春秋戰國：社會與生產
第十一章 春秋戰國：城市的發展
第十二章 結語
courses.feedback_evaluation:

- Visiting Committee report

- Reflection of teachers

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Revolution and Modernization in Twentieth-Century China
courses.description: This course examines the major political, social, economic, and cultural transformations in China from the 1920s to the late 1970s in the process of modern state-building, revolution, and modernization.
courses.learning_outcomes:
修畢本科後，學生當能：
1. 認識本世紀二十年代至七十年代末中國在建立現代國家、革命及現代化過程中所經歷的政治、經濟、社會及文化上的重大變化。

2. 掌握中國現代史的發展脈絡及對重要問題作出批判分析。

3. 了解有關中國現代史的各種概念、方法、史料、研究現況及重要著述。

4. 對民族主義與革命/現代化的課題作出研判。

5. 通過口頭和文字報告表述以上有關史識。

6. 在作業中展示對中國現代史的綜合、說明和解釋能力以及研究潛力。
courses.course_syllabus:
講授重點
本科環繞民族主義基調與革命/現代化雙重變奏的主題，討論廿世紀二十年代至七十年代中國在現代國家建構、革命及現代化過程中所經歷的重大變化。共分十三節，涉及民國時期的國家與社會關係、意識形態及文化的轉化、群眾運動、帝國主義侵略、內戰及政權轉移、中華人民共和國的建立、國家控制與社會改造、革命與現代化的矛盾，以及共產政權下中國社會的轉化等問題。除發展脈絡的闡述及重要問題的分析外，本科尚注重各種概念、方法、史料、研究現況及重要著述的評介。

講授專題
1. 緒言：民族主義、革命、現代化

2. 意識及文化的轉化：二十年代的中國

3. 十年建國：南京政府的建立及國民黨治下的社會經濟

4. 由城市至鄉村的革命：共產主義運動及江西與延安政權

5. 帝國主義衝擊與中國的戰時建國：日本侵華與中國抗戰

6. 權力角鬥與求變呼聲：國共內戰與“解放”前夕的社會

7. (放錄影帶及中期討論)

8. 國家控制與社會改造：共產政權的鞏固與社會主義過渡

9. 社會主義現代化：“大躍進”與“人民公社”

10. 危機出現與矛盾轉化：六十年代初的“兩條路線鬥爭”

11. 繼續革命的理想及其挫折：“文化大革命”

12. 毛澤東時代的總結：“四人幫”及“文革”後期的發展

13. 總結：民族主義基調與革命/現代化的雙重變奏

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: History of the Byzantine Empire

meetings.instructor: Professor James Deas David Jack MORTON
Professor James Deas David Jack MORTON

courses.description: This course will introduce students to the Byzantine Empire, the modern name that we use to refer to the Eastern Roman Empire in the Middle Ages. Beginning with its origins in the late Roman period, it will cover the story of Byzantium up to its eventual destruction by the Ottoman Turks in 1453. In addition to covering narrative history, the course will also introduce students to several key themes and ideas from Byzantine culture and society such as law, religion, art, and literature. By studying both primary sources and modern scholarship, students will develop a firm understanding of Byzantine history and an appreciation of its place within the wider history of world empires.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Learn the key facts and details of the history of the Byzantine Empire in the period 527-1453.

2. Understand and be able to debate how and why historians have constructed narratives around the changing character of the Byzantine Empire.

3. Be able to compare and contrast the Byzantine Empire with other contemporary world empires such as China.

4. Develop skills in critically analysing and interpreting primary sources relating to the history of the Byzantine Empire.

1. 了解拜占庭帝國在公元527至1453年間的重要歷史

2. 了解並能夠討論歷史學家為何及如何就拜占庭帝國不斷變化的特徵展開論述

3. 能夠將拜占庭帝國與世界其他帝國（如中國）比較

4. 培養學生批判性地分析和解釋拜占庭帝國的原始資料
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction: Byzantium in World History

2. Justinian's Empire

3. Crisis of the Seventh Century

4. Icons and Ideology: The Isaurians

5. The Macedonian 'Renaissance'

6. From Retreat to Reconquest

7. Middle Byzantine Culture and Society

8. Byzantium and the Crusades

9. The Komnenian Restoration

10. The Fourth Crusade and Its Aftermath

11. The Palaiologan Reconquest

12. Late Byzantine Culture and Society

13. Epilogue: Fall of Constantinople, End of Byzantium?

1. 引言：世界歷史中的拜占庭帝國

2. 查士丁尼一世的帝國

3. 七世紀的危機

4. 偶像與意識形態：伊蘇利亞人

5. 馬其頓的「文藝復興」

6. 從撒退到再征服

7. 拜占庭中期的文化與社會

8. 拜占庭與十字軍東征

9. 科穆寧中興

10. 第四次十字軍東征及其影響

11. 巴列奧略王朝的重新征服

12. 拜占庭晚期的文化與社會

13. 結語：君士坦丁堡的陷落，拜占庭的終結？

- Course questionnaire

1. Nathanael Aschenbrenner and Jake Ransohoff, The Invention of Byzantium in Early Modern Europe (Washington, D.C., 2021), Introduction.

2. Fiona K. Haarer, Justinian: Empire and Society in the Sixth Century (Edinburgh, 2022).

3. John F. Haldon, Byzantium in the Seventh Century: The Transformation of a Culture, rev. ed. (Cambridge, 2003).

4. Leslie Brubaker, Inventing Byzantine Iconoclasm (London, 2012).

5. Helen C. Evans and William D. Wixom (edd.), The Glory of Byzantium: Art and Culture of the Middle Byzantine Era, AD 843-1261 (New York, 1997).

6. John F. Haldon, The Byzantine Wars (Stroud, 2008).

7. James Howard-Johnston (ed.), Social Change in Town and Country in Eleventh-Century Byzantium (Oxford, 2020).

8. Jonathan Harris, Byzantium and the Crusades, 3rd ed. (London, 2022).

9. Paul Magdalino, The Empire of Manuel I Komnenos, 1143-1180 (Cambridge, 1993).

10. Michael Angold, The Fourth Crusade: Event and Context (New York, 2014).

11. Dimiter Angelov, Imperial Ideology and Political Thought in Byzantium, 1204-1330 (Cambridge, 2007).

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: History of Hong Kong
courses.description: This course mainly explores various aspects of the history of Hong Kong over the past 180 years, with a focus on analyzing the historical significance of British colonial rule, interactions between Hong Kong and mainland China, as well as local Chinese society and local identity. In addition to Chinese and English monographs and academic articles from different disciplines, students will have ample opportunities both inside and outside the classroom to engage with and analyze various types of historical sources, such as British official documents, newspaper materials, memoirs, images, videos, and oral histories. Upon completing this course, students will have the basic skills required to conduct research in Hong Kong history.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken UGEC2945 or HIST2501
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Understand the major aspects of the historical development of Hong Kong
2. Analyze the historical significance of British colonial rule over Hong Kong
3. Understand the connections and interactions between Hong Kong and mainland China over the past 180 years
4. Trace the evolution of local Chinese society and local identity
5. Introduce major Chinese and English works on Hong Kong history
6. Cultivate students’ basic abilities in Hong Kong historical research

1. 掌握1841 年起香港歷史發展的主要脈絡
2. 闡析殖民管治於香港的歷史意義
3. 了解過去百多年香港與內地的連繫與互動
4. 追溯本地華人社會和本土意識的演變
5. 介紹香港史的主要中英文著述
6. 培養學生香港史研究的基本能力

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Hong Kong History in the Field
courses.description: This course will introduce the history of Hong Kong by focusing on its “fields”. It will examine how various field sites (such as temples, museums, historical trails, and renovated historical buildings) could be used to understand the history of different places, events, and people in Hong Kong from the ancient period to the present. After a brief introduction to the historical development of Hong Kong, the course will discuss aspects of rural Hong Kong history before moving on urban Hong Kong. The last section of the course will explain the diversity of Hong Kong’s communities, cultures, and religions. To help students understand Hong Kong, the course will organize several day trips to Hong Kong Island, Kowloon Peninsula, and the New Territories.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for HIstory Majors. Not for student who have taken HIST2500/ HIST2701.
courses.learning_outcomes:
- Learn Hong Kong history from field visits

- Understand the basic structure of Hong Kong society and culture

- Rethink the historical roots of contemporary Hong Kong

- Appreciate the position of Hong Kong in Chinese history and world history

- 從田野考察中學習香港歷史

- 了解香港社會和文化的基本結構

- 反思當代香港的歷史根源

- 理解香港在中國史和世界史中的地位
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Hong Kong History: An Introduction
2. Archaeological Sites of Hong Kong
3. Forts and Cannons in Hong Kong
4. Lineages and Temples in Rural Hong Kong
5. Walled Villages in the New Territories
6. Hong Kong Fisheries
7. Remnants of Hong Kong’s Victoria City
8. Historical Trails in Urban Hong Kong
9. The Tung Wah Hospitals and Coffin Home
10. Hong Kong under Japanese Occupation: Historical Sites and Memories
11. Museums and Revitalized Historical Buildings
12. Non-Chinese Communities in Hong Kong
13. Religions and Cultures in Hong Kong

1. 香港史：引言
2. 香港的考古遺址
3. 香港的堡壘與炮台
4. 香港鄉村的宗族和祠廟
5. 新界圍村
6. 香港漁業
7. 香港維多利亞城的遺跡
8. 香港市區的史蹟徑
9. 東華醫院與義莊
10. 日治下的香港：歷史遺址和記憶
11. 博物館與活化歷史建築
12. 香港的非華人群體
13. 香港的宗教與文化

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Daily Life in Han China
courses.description: This course examines the everyday life of various classes in Han China (BCE 206 - 220 CE), with emphasis on life in the cities, occult powers, marriage customs, funeral rites, etc.
courses.learning_outcomes:
- Understand the basic development of Han society through the study of the important aspects of Chinese life during the period of Han Dynasty.

- Develop critical thinking by examining some of the conventional wisdoms in the study of Chinese history.

- Grasp presentation skills through discussion in tutorials.

- Learn to use the solid knowledge of standard histories and unearthed documents, such as bamboo slips and wooden tablets.

- 透過探討漢代人們的重要生活狀況以瞭解漢代社會的基本發展。

- 透過考察中國歷史的問題以培養批判思維。

- 透過導修課學習書面及口頭報告的技巧。

- 學習使用正史和相關出土文書的知識。
courses.course_syllabus:
Introduction: Image, Fresco, Sculpture and Daily Life

Chapter 1: Social Distinctions and Four Peoples (Statesmen, Farmers, Craftsmen, and Merchants)

Chapter 2: Farmer’s Livelihood

Chapter 3: Life in the Cities: Wine and Urban Culture

Chapter 4: Neighbors and Dwellings

Chapter 5: A Discussion on the Ri Shu (Almanacs)

Chapter 6: Sacrifices and Occult Powers

Chapter 7: Dream Interpretation and Physiognomy

Chapter 8: Festivals and Food Culture

Chapter 9: The Han Tombs in Lei Cheng Uk and Helinge'er

Chapter 10: Childhood and Education

序言： 圖像、丹青、雕塑與生活
第一章 社會階層與四民

第九章 李鄭屋村漢墓與和林格爾漢墓
第十章 漢代兒童的生活與教育

1. Ch’üT’ung-tsu, *Han Social Structure*, Chapter 4, “Social Classes”, Seattle:University of Washington Press, 1972, pp63-159.

* *第二章****農業生活**

1. Hsu Cho-yun, *Han Agriculture: The Formation of Early Chinese Agrarian Economy (206 B.C. – A.D. 220)*. Seattle and London: University of Washington Press, 1980; 或許倬雲著，王勇譯：《漢代農業——中國農業經濟的起源及特性》，桂林：廣西師範大學出版社，2005年。

1. 劉志遠：〈漢代市井考〉，載《文物》，1973年第3期。

* *第五章****《日書》探索**

2. 李學勤：〈《日書》和楚秦社會〉、〈《日書》中的《艮山圖》〉、〈《日書》盜者章研究〉，皆見氏著《簡帛佚籍與學術史》，臺北：時報文化，1994年，頁145-172。

* *第六章****祀典與怪神**

1. 《風俗通義》卷八〈祠典〉

2. 《風俗通義》卷九〈祠典〉

* *第七章****夢與相**

1. 京房撰、王照圓輯：《夢書》，見《諸子集成補編》（中華諸子寶藏‧初輯），成都：四川人民出版社，1997年。（NA B125 .C49 1997 v.7）

2. 王符：《潛夫論》卷七〈相列〉及卷八〈夢列〉，王符著、汪繼培箋、彭鐸校正：《潛夫論箋校正》（新編諸子集成．第1輯），北京：中華書局，1985年。

* *第八章****歲時與飲食**

1. 黎虎主編：《漢唐飲食文化史》，第六、七章，〈食制與食俗〉，〈飲食與社會〉，北京：北京師範大學出版社，1998年，頁253-329。

2. 黃展岳：〈從出土文物看南越王的飲食〉、〈漢代人的飲食生活〉，見氏著《先秦兩漢考古與文化》，臺北：允晨文化，1999年，頁267-270及頁474-486。

* *第九章****李鄭屋村漢墓與和林格爾漢墓**

1. 香港歷史博物館編製：《李鄭屋漢墓》，香港：香港歷史博物館，2005年。

1. 王子今：〈兩漢童蒙教育〉，載《史學集刊》，2007年第3期，頁15-24。

2. 王子今：〈漢代兒童的游藝生活〉，載王子今，《秦漢社會史論考》(北京：商務印書館，2006年)，頁1-18。

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Regional Cultures in Han China
courses.description: This course examines the local cultures of various geographical regions, with the emphasis on the process of cultural interaction and integration under the rule of Han Empire (BCE 206-220 CE).
courses.learning_outcomes:
- Understand the local cultures of various geographical regions during the rule of the Han Empire (BCE 206-220 CE).

- Develop critical thinking by examining some of the conventional wisdoms in the study of Chinese history.

- Grasp presentation skills through discussion in tutorials.

- Learn to use the solid knowledge of standard histories.

- 瞭解漢代時期（公元206至220年）各地區的文化傳統。

- 透過考察中國歷史的問題以培養批判思維。

- 透過導修課學習書面及口頭報告的技巧。

- 學習使用正史的知識。
courses.course_syllabus:
第一章 緒論：生態環境與文化區域
第二章 漢代文化區域及文化重心
第三章 齊魯文化區
第四章 濱海文化區
第五章 河洛文化區
第六章 漢代長安及關中文化
第七章 北邊區的軍事文化
第八章 漢代滇人的社會生活
第九章 地方意識成長
第十章 城市發展與區域文化
第十一章 結論：文化整合的詮釋

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Society in Late Imperial China
courses.description: This course looks at the various stabilizing and subversive forces in Ming-Qing society, and how or why modern scholars evaluate this history very differently. Emphasis will also be on the question of the individual and society, from the perspectives of gender issues and health and medicine.
courses.learning_outcomes:
- understanding history in relation to contemporary Chinese issues

- appreciating complex social and political processes

- understanding the relation between the individual and society

- improving writing skills

- 通過明清歷史的脈絡來掌握當代中國的各種問題；

- 理解明清時期的各種重大社會、政治事件

- 思考個人與社會關係

- 改善寫作技巧
courses.course_syllabus:
(1) Introduction

(2) Son of Heaven vis-a-vis commoners: class in Ming-Qing era

(3) Man and woman: gender in Ming-Qing era

(4) Children in Ming-Qing era

(5) Silver and copper cash: the economy of Ming-Qing era

(6) The evolution of taxation system in Ming-Qing era

(7) The lineage of Ming-Qing era

(8) The imperial civil examination system

(9) Material life in Ming-Qing era: clothing

(10) Material life in Ming-Qing era: food

(11) Material life in Ming-Qing era: housing

(12) Material life in Ming-Qing era: transport

(13) Conclusion

(1) 序論

(2) 天子與庶民：明清時期的階級

(3) 男人與女人：明清時期的性別問題

(4) 幼吾幼以及人之幼：明清時期的兒童

(5) 白銀與銅錢：明清時期的經濟

(6) 里甲、一條鞭法、攤丁入地：明清時期賦役制度的變化

(7) 光宗耀祖：明清時期的宗族

(8) 萬般皆下品惟有讀書高：明清時期的科舉制度

(9) 衣食住行：明清時期的物質生活(i)

(10) 衣食住行：明清時期的物質生活(ii)

(11) 衣食住行：明清時期的物質生活(iii)

(12) 衣食住行：明清時期的物質生活(iv)

(13) 結論

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Chinese Maritime Connections in Asia, 1500-1800
courses.description: This course aims to show how the passages of Chinese people, ships, commodities, and ideas connected different maritime regions in East and Southeast Asia and other parts of the world in the early modern era (c. 1500-1800). It takes into account the state policies of China, other Asian “indigenous” polities, and European “imperialists,” and the activities of different Chinese groups such as merchants, sailors, pirates, and settlers in China, Japan, and Southeast Asia. Assisted by a variety of textual, cartographic, and pictorial sources, the course explores the roles of various forms of Chinese circulations such as tribute trade, smuggling, and migration in forming an Asian macro-region with its own intrinsic political, social, economic, and cultural orders. Students will have much chance to read historical materials and secondary literature in both English and Chinese.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Having taken this course, students should be able to:

• Understand the various ways in which the circulations of Chinese people, ships, commodities, and ideas linked different regions of maritime Asia and other parts of the world from 1500 to 1800;
• Weave Chinese maritime history into East Asian history, Southeast Asian history, and the history of European empires, and understand maritime history as an analytical approach to world history, regional history, and local history
• Appreciate the historical roots of contemporary links across maritime Asia such as migration, trading networks, and cultural flows; and
• Engage in academic discussions and analyze primary sources on specific historical issues related to the Chinese presence in maritime Asia during the lectures and tutorial lessons and in a variety of written assignments.

完成本課程後，學生應能夠：

• 理解1500年至1800年間華人及其船隻、商品和思想的流動如何將亞洲不同的海洋地區與世界其他地方聯繫起來；
• 將中國海洋史融入東亞歷史、東南亞歷史和歐洲帝國史，並理解海洋史作爲研究世界史、區域史和地方史的研究方法；
• 從移民、貿易網絡和文化流動等議題理解當代亞洲海洋地區各地聯繫的歷史根源；
• 在講課、導修課以及不同作業中參與學術討論並分析中國與海洋亞洲相關的歷史問題。
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction: Conceptualizing Maritime Asia
2. The Ming-Qing Maritime Policies
3. Maritime Policies in Japan and Southeast Asia

13. Historical Methods in Chinese Maritime History

1. 導論：「海洋亞洲」的概念
2. 明清時期的海洋政策

4. 朝貢制度與朝貢貿易

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Social and Cultural History of Modern China Cities
courses.description: This course explores the social and cultural history of Chinese cities from the late Qing to early People's Republic. It examines not only the material space and built environment of major cities but also the symbolism, economic activities and everyday life of their residents. Themes such as urban planning, print culture, the film industry, literary representations, commodification, public space, gender relations and political activism will be examined.
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Understand the importance of cities in modern Chinese history
* Appreciate the many ways in which urban spaces were built, used and represented
* Develop critical thinking through the analysis of historical materials
* Deepen the understanding of Chinese cultures through primary sources
* Learn basic research skills of conducting historical research and enhance writing skills

* 明白城市在現代中國歷史中的獨特性
* 理解城市空間建構、使用及再現的多樣性
* 透過分析史料建立批判思維
* 透過原始資料加深對中國文化的認識
* 學習撰寫歷史研究報告的基礎知識從而改善寫作能力

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: History of Modern Europe
courses.description: The course surveys Europe's "Long" (19th) and "Short" (20th) Centuries, i.e. from the outbreak of the French Revolution (1789) to the fall of the Berlin Wall (1989). It will detail the major political, economic, philosophical, and social events of this period and will examine them as a series of interwoven threads rather than as separate events. This period gave full vent to the forces of industrialization, capitalism, nationalism, imperialism, and communism while it provided the conditions for artistic innovation, philosophical competition, and scientific advancement. During these 200 years, as a continent Europe achieved its height in military, cultural, and commercial power; it then engaged in a period of violent self-destruction; finally, after profound ideological and territorial division, it reinvented itself. How the scope and volatility of the changes brought about during this time manifested themselves in the lives of ordinary men, women, and children, as well as in the affairs among nations, will receive due attention. Readings for the course will hear from many scholarly voices and will engage with a variety of primary sources that should be viewed as historical texts that open windows onto inquiries into the nature of class, gender, minority status, religion, technology, and culture.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken HIST4306
courses.learning_outcomes: With our emphasis on critical thinking, essay writing, and primary source interpretation, not only will students be profoundly better informed as to the subject matter and demonstrably more expert on matters of historical investigation; you will also leave with an enhanced set of skills. After successfully completing the coursework, students will be better able to express ideas clearly in writing, to conduct scholarly research, to analyze information and to critique differing viewpoints, to compare and to contrast contrary cases of historical interpretation, to discern and to explain the links between historical events, and to assess the importance of historical documents or of cultural artifacts whether they comprise a political cartoon, book, painting, speech, song, or film.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week One: Vive la (French) Révolution

Week Two: Napoleon and Europe’s Restoration

Week Three: A World of Cities and Smoke (The Industrial Revolution)

Tutorial One, Reverberations of the Twin Revolutions

Week Four: Intangibles (Competing Philosophies)

Week Five: Out of Chaos, Consolidation (Building Nation-states)

Tutorial Two: The Circumstances of Ordinary People

Week Six: The White Man’s Burden (Imperialism)

Week Seven: Industrialism, Exuberance, and Ennui

Tutorial Three: The Citizen in the Modern Nation State

Week Eight: War, Revolution, and an Uneasy Peace

Week Nine: Recovery amid Uncertainty

Week Ten: The Rise of Authoritarianism

Week Eleven: War and a Continent Divided

Tutorial Four: Ich bin ein Berliner

Week Twelve: Addition (Prosperity) and Subtraction (Decolonization) in the West

Week Thirteen: A New European Order

courses.required_readings:
Week One: Vive la (French) Révolution:

Required Reading: Mortimer Chambers et al, The Western Experience, Chapter 20: ‘The French Revolution’, (Boston: McGraw Hill, 2007), 583-611.

Take a listen:La Marseillaise, [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4K1q9Ntcr5g](%20http:/www.youtube.com/watch?v=4K1q9Ntcr5g)

Week Two: Napoleon and Europe’s Restoration Mortimer Chambers et al, Chapter 21: ‘The Age of Napoleon,’ The Western Experience, 615-638.

Required Reading:Robin Winks, Chapter One: “Restoration and Revolution,’ Europe and the Making of Modernity, 1815-1914, (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2005), 11-40.

Week Three:A World of Cities and Smoke (The Industrial Revolution)

Required Reading:Robin Winks, Chapter 3: “The Industrial Age Begins,” Europe and the Making of Modernity, 1815-1914, 64-92.

Take a look:Excerpts from The Sadler Report.

Tutorial One, Reverberations of the Twin Revolutions

Primary reading (excerpts from):Edmund Burke, Reflections on the Revolution in France; Maximilien Robespierre, Justification of the Use of Terror, and the Carlsbad Decrees.

Complementary reading:Clark Nardinelli, “Child Labor and the Factory Acts.” The Journal of Economic History 40:4 (December 1980):739-755.

Week Four: Intangibles (Competing Philosophies)

Required Reading:Robin Winks, Chapter 2: “Romanticism,’ 41-63; Chapter 5: “Ideas and Ideologies,” Europe and the Making of Modernity, 1815-1914, 125-152.

Take look and a listen:Romanticism: Revolt of the Spirit (clip), <http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=18F56JWeSr4>;

Franz Schubert, Symphony No. 8 in B Minor (Unfinished), II(2 of 2), <http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xBqSqCkk418>;

WilliamTurner, “Vesuvius Eruption” (1817), Alexander Pushkin, “To the Emperor Nicholas I” (1826), Lord Byron, “My Soul Is Dark” (1815), and P. B. Shelley, “The Call to Freedom” (1819)

Week Five: Out of Chaos, Consolidation (Building Nation-states)

Required Reading:Robin Winks, Chapter 7: “The Modern Nation State,” Europe and the Making of Modernity, 1815-1914, 183-228.

Take a look:Map of Europe and the revolutions of 1848.

Take a listen:Giuseppe Verdi, the “Chorus of the Hebrews” from the opera, [Nabucco](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nabucco "Nabucco"), <http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TLkYfppYUwc> ;

And

Richard Wagner, "The Ride of the Valkyries" from the opera, Die Walkure, <http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9RpDhX2CHLE>

Tutorial Two:The Circumstances of Ordinary People

Primary Reading:Excerpts from the French Civil Code, the Act to Emancipate the Serfs of Russia, A Letter to Queen Victoria on Lord Chancellor Cranworth's Marriage and Divorce Bill, and Chapter One of Honore de Balzac’s Cousin Bette.

Complementary Reading:Steven L. Hoch,“Did Russia’s Emancipated Serfs Really Pay too Much for too Little Land?Statistical Anomalies and Long-Tailed Distributions,” Slavic Review 63: 2, (2004): 247-274.

Week Six: The White Man’s Burden (Imperialism)

Required Reading:Robin Winks, Chapter 9: “The Age of Imperialism,” Europe and the Making of Modernity, 1815-1914, 257-288.

Take a look: Rudyard Kipling, “The White Man’s Burden.”

Week Seven:Industrialism, Exuberance, and Ennui

Required Readings:Robin Winks, Chapter 8: “Reason, Realism, and Respectability,” 229-257, and Chapter 10: “The Challenges of Modernity,” Europe and the Making of Modernity, 1815-1914, 289-318.

Take a look:The Impressionists (Pre- and Post-)

Assignment #2 due:Mid-term essay submitted.

Tutorial Three:The Citizen in the Modern Nation State

Primary Viewing:Excerpt from Oscar Wilde, An Ideal Husband (Part 1 of 10), <http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uzS-JSqIBag>

Primary Reading: Peter Kropotkin, Excerpts from, Anarchism, Its Philosophy and Ideal.

Complementary Reading:Immanuel Wallerstein,“Citizens All? Citizens Some! The Making of the Citizen,” Comparative Studies in Society and History 45:4 (2003): 650-679.

Week Eight: War, Revolution, and an Uneasy Peace

Required Reading:Thomas F. X. Noble et al, “War and Revolution,” in Western Civilization, The Continuing Experiment, (Boston: Houghton Mifflin Company, 2005): 843-878.

Take a look: All Quiet on the Western Front (Part One) <http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fbArOFsXs6I>

Week Nine:Recovery amid Uncertainty

Required Reading:Thomas F. X. Noble et al, “The Illusion of Stability,” 879-912.

Take a look:Josephine Baker's Banana Dance, <http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wmw5eGh888Y>

Take a listen:T. S. Eliot reading “Part 1: The Burial of the Dead,” from The Waste Land.<http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3tqK5zQlCDQ>

Week Ten:The Rise of Authoritarianism

Required Reading:Thomas F. X. Noble et al, “The Tortured Decade,” 913-946.

Take a look:Pablo Picasso’s, “Guernica.”<http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eKVCov-XFXw>

Week Eleven: War and a Continent Divided

Required Reading:Thomas F. X. Noble et al, “The Era of the Second World War,” 947-984

Take a look:Winston Churchill’s “Iron Curtain” speech, 5 March 1946.<http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jvax5VUvjWQ>

Tutorial Four:Ich bin ein Berliner

Primary Readings:USSR General Secretary Nikita Khrushchev and USA President John F. Kennedy speeches, <http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Dj9KNPpUr0c>

Complementary Reading:Johanna Granville, “Radio Free Europe’s Impact on the Kremlin in the Hungarian Crisis of 1956: Three Hypothesis.”Canadian Journal of History [Canada] 39:3 (2004):515-546.

Week Twelve:Addition (Prosperity) and Subtraction (Decolonization) in the West

Required Reading:John Merriman, Chapter 28, “Decolonization and a New Prosperity,” from A History of Modern Europe from the Renaissance to the Present, (New York; London: W. W. Norton & Company, 2004):1251-1287.

Take a look and a listen:Bob Marley, Get Up, Stand Up, <http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=q7iXcKKpdx0>

Week Thirteen:A New European Order

Required Viewing:Goodbye Lenin.(Organized class viewing to be scheduled)

Required Reading:John Merriman, Chapter 29, “The Emergence of Contemporary Europe and the Collapse of Communism,” from A History of Modern Europe from the Renaissance to the Present, 1288-1342.

Assignment #3 due:Submit final essay.

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Europe's Descent: World War II and the Holocaust
courses.description: This course will cover the rise of Fascism and Nazism in the 1930s, the military history of World War II in Europe and the Atlantic, and the Holocaust. Equal balance will be given to the military history of the war in Europe, and the Holocaust.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To teach students the history of the Holocaust and of World War II in Europe: key people, developments, battles, and how the mass killings became possible.
2. To familiarize students with philosophical and historiographical debates about the Holocaust, including those which the Nuremberg Trials had to wrestle with.
3. To understand classic and current debates in Genocide Studies.

1. 同學將能夠把握第二次世界大戰期間納粹德國對於猶太人進行的大屠殺事件，包括主要人物、主要事件、戰役、及大屠殺之執行。
2. 同學將了解有關猶太人大屠殺的哲學與歷史學辯論，包括二戰結束後紐倫堡審訊期間的論辯。
3、 同學將能夠理解有關種族滅絕的過去及現在的論辯。

11. Liberating the camps

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: History of Modern Britain
courses.description: This course examines British history from Victorian period to the twentieth century. It will detail the major political, economic, philosophical, artistic, scientific, and social events of this period and will examine them as a series of interwoven threads rather than as separate events. Consider that during the span of our investigation this small island state and its collection of nationalities came to exert control and influence over a quarter of the world's land mass and for much of the time operated as unchallenged mistress of the seas. The course proceeds as an empirical examination of the progress of multiple facets of British history, but all along our inspection we will confront the question as to what it meant (means) to be British.
courses.learning_outcomes: Students are able to have a full grip of modern British history after this course. They will be able to express ideas clearly and to analyze information and to critique differing viewpoints by the end of this course. With the knowledge of modern British history and the development of the British Empire, students are able to understand the globalization in the beginning of the century, which enhances their values of Global Citizenship in the modern time.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction – Victorian Britain

2. War and Empire

3. Gladstone and Disraeli- British High Imperialism

4. The Great War

5. National Government and the Great Depression

6. Totalitarianism and Appeasement

7. Churchill and WWII

8. Swinging London and the 60s

9. Commonwealth and the Cold War

10. The Road to Welfare State

11. Irish Question

12. From Thatcher to Blair

13. Contemporary Britain: Who are the British?

courses.required_readings:
Lecture 1: Introduction – Victorian Britain

Walter L. Arnstein, Chapter 2: “The Railway Age,” 18-29, in Britain Yesterday and Today: 1830 to the Present, (Lexington, MA: D. C. Heath and Company, 1988); W. J. Reader, Chapter 1: “The Victorian Setting,” in Life in Victorian England, (London: William Clowes and Sons, Limited, 1964): 1-16.

Lecture 2: War and Empire

John F.Richards, “Opium and the British Indian Empire: The Royal Commission of 1895”, Modern Asian Studies 36:2 (2002): 375-420.

Lecture 3: Gladstone and Disraeli- British High Imperialism

Walter L. Arnstein, Chapter 8: “Disraeli and Gladstone,” in Britain Yesterday and Today: 1830 to the Present: 127-15.

Lecture 4: The Great War

Walter L. Arnstein, Chapter 14, “Britain and World War I,” in Britain Yesterday and Today: 1830 to the Present: 246-262.

Lecture 5: National Government and the Great Depression

Walter L. Arnstein, Chapter 16: "The False Dawn and the Great Depression," in Britain Yesterday and Today: 1830 to the Present: 285-305.

Lecture 6: Totalitarianism and Appeasement

Walter L. Arnstein, Chapter 17: “The Storm Gathers Anew,” in Britain Yesterday and Today: 1830 to the Present: 306-318.

Lecture 7: Churchill and WWII

Walter L. Arnstein, Chapter 18, “The Age of Churchill,” in Britain Yesterday and Today: 1830 to the Present: 319-339.

Lecture 8: Swinging London and the 60s

Walter L. Arnstein, Chapter 20, “The Decline of Empire and the Mood of the 1960s,” in Britain Yesterday and Today: 1830 to the Present: 367-393.

Lecture 9: Commonwealth and the Cold War

Kenneth Morgan, The people’s peace: British history 1945-1989, (Oxford, 1990): 197-239.

Lecture 10: The Road to Welfare State

“Social Insurance and Allied Services Report” by Sir William Beveridge, Presented to Parliament by Command of His Majesty November 1942.

Lecture 11: Irish Question

Niall Ó Dochartaigh, “ Northern Ireland since 1920” in R. Bourke and I. McBride (eds.) The Princeton history of modern Ireland, (Princeton, 2016): 141-167.

Lecture 12: From Thatcher to Blair

Rebecca Fraser, “The Sick Man of Europe” and “The Thatcher Legacy,” in A People's History of Britain, (London: Chatto & Windus, 2003): 744-785.

Lecture 13: Contemporary Britain: Who are the British?

Gerry Smyth, “Ethnicity and Language,” in M. Storry and P. Childs (eds.) British Cultural Identities, (Oxon, 2013): 205-232.

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: The Philippines and Trans Pacific Connections
courses.description: HIST3375 offers an examination of the evolution of Philippine society from the onset of Spanish colonization in the mid-1500s to the start of World War Two in Asia (in 1941). Granting an opportunity to grasp the development of the Philippines under Spanish, American, and Japanese colonial rule, the course places attention upon the nation’s capital city, Manila, a place where life has also been historically shaped by Chinese, Japanese, Western, and native cultural influences. Focusing on elements of cultural, political, and urban history the course endeavours to explain how ‘the Philippine nation’ has historically unfolded, and why Manila, today a metropolis with a population in excess of 13 million people, has come to play such a dominant role in determining the nature of Philippine society prior to the country being granted independence in 1946. Utilizing innovative teaching methods to enable students to critically understand the physical as well as cultural character of the city, and so the nation at large, a multidisciplinary approach is fostered so as to encourage learners to recognize and explain factors that have influenced Philippines civilization as it progressed from the “traditional” to the “modern”. Encouraging learners too to identify connections between the country and places elsewhere in the Asia-Pacific region, ultimately the course places the evolution of life in the Philippine Archipelago within the greater Asia-Pacific region. Accordingly, the curriculum is cut into two distinct sections: The Philippines pre-1898; and, the Philippines post-1898. Where possible a comparative analysis with the colonial development of Hong Kong will be provided so as to demonstrate, and explain, the different types and natures of colonial rule, culture, and development that took place in East Asia prior to the end of World War Two.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introductory class.
2. The arrival of the Spanish.
3. Building the State: Spanish colonisation and ethnic groups.
4. Spanish Manila: America's First Chinatown.
5. The Rise and Ending of the Galleon Trade.
6. Understanding the Binondo.
7. Nationalism inside the Spanish Empire.
8. American Perceptions of the Philippines and the Building of the American Colonial State.
9. Redesigning Manila: The Role of City BEautiful Urbanism.
10. Modernity in East Asia.
11. The Virtual Fieldtrip, and Filipinization and the Widening Structure of the Colonial Government.
12. The Road to Independence.
13. The Post-Colonial and the Legacies of the Colonial Ages.

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Modern East Asian Political and Cultural History
courses.description: Focusing on East Asia, this subject explores the interactions and interconnectedness of Asian societies since the nineteenth century. It discusses how political figures, intellectuals and states understood and leveraged "Asia" to establish their subject positions and negotiate the international order in which they found themselves to be situated. Recognizing the multiplicities and complexity of "Asia" as a conceptual category, this course prods participates to contemplate the evolving power relations that undergirded the region and beyond. They are also encouraged to consider the potential and challenges of reconciliation and synergy within the region.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students should be able to:

1. Appreciate the complexity and indeterminateness of "Asia."
2. Comprehend the ideals and power dynamics that inform inter-regional imaginings.
3. Leverage literary and visual works for historical research.
4. Conduct independent research in the form of an extended essay.

學生該能夠：

1. 瞭解「亞洲」意涵的複雜性和浮動性
2. 掌握各種跨地域想像背後的動機和權力關係
3. 利用文藝作品及理論性較強的論述性文獻輔助歷史研究
4. 以學期論文體裁進行獨立研究
courses.course_syllabus:
Session 1: What is/was Asia?
Session 2: Tributary system: A Sinocentric regional order

Session 3: Western imperialism and the rise of the nation-state system

Session 4: Leaving Asia?: the rise of Japan and Pan-Asianism

Session 5: The Chinese revolution and its Asian connections

Session 6: Sino-Japanese connections

Session 7: Colonial Taiwan: searching for an identity

Session 8: Manchukuo and the Great East-Asia Co-prosperity Sphere

Session 9: Renewed encounters between China and India

Session 10: Realignments within Asia under the Cold War

Session 11: America's Asia: from "Asia-Pacific" to "Indo-Pacific"

Session 12: Historical memories and popular culture

Session 13: Attempts at reconciliation

第一課　何謂亞洲？

第二課　朝貢體系：中華世界的區域秩序

第三課　西方帝國主義及民族國家體系的興起

第四課　脫亞？：日本的興起及大亞洲主義

第五課　中國革命的亞洲連結

第六課　中日互動

第七課　殖民地台灣知識份子的身份認同

第八課　滿州國及大東亞共榮圈

第九課　中國與印度的再遇

第十課　冷戰下亞洲的分裂與聯合

第十一課　美國的亞洲：從「亞太」到「印太」

第十二課　戰爭記憶與流行文化

第十三課　和解的嘗試

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Historical Literature and Documents
courses.description: This course is designed to expose students to the style, sources and structure of prominent ancient works of Chinese and/or Western history and historical documents, and to provide guidance in reading, comprehension and/or translation. Offerings with different subtitles can be taken up for credit, up to a maximum of two times.
courses.learning_outcomes:
At the end of the course students should be able to:

- understand the style, sources and structure of prominent ancient works of Chinese and/or Western history and historical documents.

- enhance their ability in reading, comprehending and/or translating prominent ancient works of Chinese and /or Western History and historical documents.

學生畢業本科後，應能：
- 了解古代（中國及／或西方）史書及文獻之體例、取材及結構。

- 提高其閱讀、理解及／或翻譯古代文獻典籍之能力。

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: War and Society in Ancient China (from Early Period to the Sixth Century CE)
courses.description: This course aims to present and explain how war and society shaped each other in classical and early medieval China, roughly from the early period to the sixth century CE. While the interactive relations between state, society, and warfare constitute the main thread that runs through the course as a whole, this course also covers a broad range of issues, including the operation of various military institutions and their rationales; civil-military relations; political and military cultures; military thoughts, tactics, and strategy; social impact of warfare, cultural responses to military conquests; intellectual debates on statecraft; as well as interactions, especially in the military aspects, between Chinese regimes and their Eurasian neighbors.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the subject, students will be able to:

a. examine how the development of ancient Chinese civilization shaped, and was shaped by, war.

b. analyze the tension and complementarity of cultural (wen) and martial (wu) values in Chinese culture and their long-lasting impact on Chinese culture.

c. reflect the impact of war on pre-modern Chinese society and on the living of common people as well.

d. discuss the relationship between war and society of ancient China in Eurasian and comparative perspectives.

e. develop a strategic perspective of viewing historical and current issues.

學員在研習本課程後，將可具備以下知識與能力：

1. 認識傳統中國文明與戰爭的互動關係

2. 分析中國傳統文化中的文與武觀念及其對中國社會與文化的深遠影響

3. 反思戰爭的禍害及其對普遍民眾生活帶來的影響

4. 從歐亞大陸歷史發展的比較角度來了解古代中國的戰爭與社會之關係

5. 透過學習軍事史而培養以戰略思維分析事物
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Why do we study ancient Chinese warfare?

2. The state of the art in military history

3. Collective violence in early societies

4. The formation of early states and the ascendancy of aristocratic warriors

5. Sanctioned militarization of pre- and early imperial Chinese society

6. Thinking about war in a chaotic era

7. The strategic culture of early imperial China

8. Military culture and regional diversity

9. The disintegration of the Han empire and the rise of militant communities

10. Spontaneous militarization of early medieval Chinese society

11. Ethnicity and warfare

12. Religious beliefs and warfare

13. Ancient Chinese warfare in a world historical context

第一講　導論：為甚麼要研究古代戰爭？

第二講　軍事史研究的一些概念、術語及學術現況

第三講　早期社會中的集體暴力

第四講　國家的建立與貴族武裝的登場

第五講　軍國體制與全方位戰爭

第六講　亂世中對暴力的思考

第七講　帝國的戰略順序

第八講　軍事文化與地域分野

第九講　帝國崩解與武將群體的冒起

第十講　暴力橫行與軍事化措施

第十一講　胡漢問題與戰爭暴力

第十二講　宗教信仰與戰爭暴力

第十三講　世界史視野下的古代中國戰爭

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Hannah Arendt between History and Philosophy
courses.description: This course investigates the work of Hannah Arendt by exploring her writings from both philosophical and historical perspectives. Students will learn about Arendt as an important political commentator and theorist of the 20th century, as well as the key philosophical concepts that underly her work. Borrowing approaches from both History and Philosophy, this course will examine the work of Hannah Arendt as both a commentor on the twentieth century, and as a participant in the longer tradition of Continental Philosophy. Students will learn about her life, her background in phenomenology, her experiences during the Second World War, and her attempts to make sense of totalitarianism by borrowing both from her own experiences and research, and from her ability to integrate past approaches to the relationship between philosophical thought and the passage of history.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who are taking or have taken PHIL3312.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students will develop competence in the essential concepts and arguments of Hannah Arendt’s works, including The Human Condition, Origins of Totalitarianism, Eichmann in Jerusalem, On Revolution, and essays from Life of the Mind.
2. Students will be able to successfully articulate and defend a philosophical, historical, or interdisciplinary thesis of their own.
3. Students will develop the capacity to think through a philosophical problem, reconstruct Arendts' arguments from her texts, and develop the capacity to construct their own critiques.
4. Students will develop a basic understanding of the relationship between Arendt's thought and the historical context of her writing, as well as the influence and legacy of her work in other philosophers.

1. 培養學生對漢娜·萼蘭特作品核心概念和論證的理解，包括《人的境況》、《極權主義的起源》、《艾希曼在耶路撒冷》、《論革命》以及《心靈生活》中的散文。
2. 學生能闡述自己提出的哲學、歷史或跨學科論題，並為之辯護。
3. 培養學生思考哲學問題的能力，重構萼蘭文本裏的論證，培養建構自己批判論證的能力。
4. 學生能基本了解萼蘭的思想與其寫作歷史背景的關係，以及其作品對其他哲學家的影響和傳承。
courses.course_syllabus:
The core of the course will be Arendt's own writings. Her most important books are The Origins of Totalitarianism; The Human Condition; On Revolution; The Life of the Mind; and Eichmann in Jerusalem. Including all of those texts is too much for one semester some collection of readings from those texts should make up the bulk of the course.

A possible semester might be:

Week 1: Introduction to Arendt
Weeks 2-3: Arendt's pre-war writings
Week 4-7: Origins of Totalitarianism
Week 8-10: The Human Condition
Week 11: The Life of the Mind or On Revolution
Week 12: Eichmann in Jerusalem
Week 13: Arendt after Arendt

課程核心將以萼蘭本人的著作為主。她最重要的書籍包括：《極權主義的起源》、《人的境況》、《論革命》、《心靈生活》以及《艾希曼在耶路撒冷》。把這些文本全部納入一個學期的課程或會太多；我們將從中節選篇章，作為本課程主要部分。

一個可能的學期安排如下：

第1週：萼蘭導論
第2-3週：萼蘭戰前著作
第4-7週：《極權主義的起源》
第8-10週：《人的境況》
第11週：《心靈生活》或《論革命》
第12週：《艾希曼在耶路撒冷》
第13週：萼蘭之後的萼蘭

courses.subject: HIST
courses.title: Museum and Heritage
courses.description: This course reviews the historical development of concepts and theories related to museums and cultural heritage. It introduces diversified definitions of museums and heritage in the contemporary. Through selected case studies and field trips, it demonstrates the operations of museums and heritage sites; and analyses their social roles in modern society. Reviewing the historical evolution of international conventions related to museums and heritage outlines how vital international and regional cooperation is for sustainable heritage conservation and management.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students should understand core concepts and theories related to museums and heritage in the contemporary world.

2. Students should learn about the historical development of museums and heritage.

3. Students shall develop their capacity to review the social roles of museums and heritage in contemporary society.

4. Students shall be able to outline the importance of international and regional cooperation on issues related to heritage conservation.

1. 學生應了解與當代博物館和文化遺產相關的主要概念和理論框架。

2. 學生應學習博物館和文化遺產概念的歷史發展和變革的過程。

3. 學生應了解博物館和文化遺產在當代社會所發揮的角色並建立自己的見解。

4. 學生應能勾勒國際及地區性合作對於文化遺產保育的重要性。
courses.course_syllabus:
Section 1: Concepts and theories related to museums and heritage

1. The origin of the museum and its historical development.

2. Core concepts related to museum curation and artefact management.

3. The history of the museums in East Asia, including mainland China and Hong Kong.

4. The concepts and definitions of cultural heritage and their historical evolution.

5. The understandings and interpretation of cultural heritage in East Asian context.

Section 2: The social roles of museums and heritage and their public engagement

1. The transformation of the social roles of museums and heritage.

2. The educational functions of museums and heritage.

3. The social inclusion of museums and heritage.

4. The reuse and revitalisation of heritage in modern society.

Section 3: Achieving sustainable heritage conservation and management through International and regional cooperation

1. International conventions related to heritage conservation and management.

2. The modus operandi of UNESCO World heritage and its sustainable development goals.

3. The challenges and opportunities of international and regional cooperation regarding heritage conservation and management.

第一部：博物館及文化遺產的概念和理論

1. 博物館概念的源起、發展和擴充。

2. 博物館策展以及文物展示的基本概念和知識。

3. 東亞地區包括中國內地和香港博物館的發展歷史。

4. 文化遺產概念的源起及發展。

5. 東亞地區對於文化遺產概念的理解和演繹。

第二部：博物館及文化遺產的社會角色與公眾參與

1. 博物館及文化遺產社會角色的歷史演變。

2. 博物館及文化遺產的教育功能和角色。

3. 博物館及文化遺產與不同社群公眾的關係和連結。

4. 文化遺產在當代社會的活化和再利用。

第三部：國際及地區性合作與可持續的文化遺產管理

1. 有關文化遺產保育和管理的國際性公約和守則。

2. 聯合國世界文化遺產的運作及管理機制；以及其可持續發展目標。

3. 國際及地區性合作在文化遺產保育上所面對挑戰及機遇。

courses.description: This course explores the interaction of natural geography, administrative geography, socio-economic transformation and major historical events in Huizhou region in China in the last millennium.

- Understand the interaction between natural environment and human activities;

- Develop critical insight on complex issues like relations between individuality and collectivity, between small community and larger organization, between society and state.
